In the survival context, the `Score()` function from the `riskRegression` package handles both the **IPCW adjustment** for censoring and the **bootstrap correction** for over-optimism simultaneously, providing a rigorous assessment on the full dataset.

The course flowchart places internal validation as the first gate: *"Is the risk prediction accurate?"* If the answer is no, the model must be revised before proceeding to external validation and clinical use.

## Part VI: Clinical Utility — "Is the Risk Prediction Useful?" (Question 10)

The final and most consequential question is not statistical but clinical: does using the model to guide treatment decisions produce **better outcomes** than simpler strategies? This is the domain of **decision curve analysis** (Vickers \& Elkin, 2006).

The key insight, developed in the course slides, is that a risk predictor $p$ becomes clinically actionable only when combined with a **treatment threshold** $r$: treat if $\hat{p} \geq r$, do not treat otherwise. Each threshold $r$ implies a cost-benefit ratio $C = r/(1-r)$, reflecting how many unnecessary treatments a clinician is willing to accept to avoid missing one case. The **Expected Net Benefit** at threshold $r$ is:

$$ENB(r) = TPF(r) \cdot \hat{\rho} - \frac{r}{1-r} \cdot FPF(r) \cdot (1 - \hat{\rho})$$

The decision curve plots $ENB(r)$ against $r$ for the model and the two reference strategies ("treat all" and "treat none"). A model is clinically useful at threshold $r$ only if its net benefit exceeds both references.

Decision curve analysis has a profound implication that the course emphasises: **a model with a good AUC can still be clinically harmful**. As noted in the Vickers et al.\ (2019) guide, if a model systematically underestimates risk (poor calibration), patients at high risk may receive reassuringly low predicted values and forgo treatment. The AUC would be unaffected --- it depends only on ranks --- but the net benefit would plummet. This is why calibration, discrimination, and net benefit must all be assessed, and why the decision curve is the ultimate arbiter of clinical value.

## The Complete Pipeline

Looking back, the 10 questions form a coherent analytical pipeline that mirrors the Steyerberg (2009) framework:

$$\underbrace{\text{Q.1--4}}_{\textbf{Describe \& Prepare}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Q.5}}_{\textbf{Model}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Q.6--7}}_{\textbf{Calibrate}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Q.8}}_{\textbf{Discriminate}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Q.9}}_{\textbf{Validate}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Q.10}}_{\textbf{Decide}}$$

Each step answers a progressively deeper question:

| Step | Question | Tools | Course Reference |
|:-----|:---------|:------|:-----------------|
| Q.1--2 | What is the survival and censoring landscape? | Kaplan-Meier, reverse KM | Slides 3--4 |
| Q.3--4 | How to handle censoring at the landmark? | Sample restriction | Slides 3 (censoring bias) |
| Q.5 | What are patient-specific predicted risks? | Cox PH model, Breslow estimator | Slides 5 |
| Q.6--7 | Is the prediction accurate? | Calibration plot, Brier Score | Slides 6 (calibration) |
| Q.8 | Is the prediction consistent in ranks? | ROC curve, AUC, Youden index | Slides 6 (discrimination) |
| Q.9 | Is the accuracy honest? | Bootstrap, IPCW | Slides 6 (validation), Gerds \& Schumacher (2006) |
| Q.10 | Is the prediction clinically useful? | Decision curve analysis, Net Benefit | Slides 6 (clinical impact), Vickers \& Elkin (2006) |

## Key Takeaways

**1. Censoring is the defining challenge of survival-based risk prediction.** Every performance metric must either be computed on a sample where the outcome is fully observed (restricted sample approach) or be adjusted for censoring (IPCW approach). Ignoring censoring leads to biased estimates.

**2. Calibration and discrimination are complementary, not interchangeable.** A model needs both to be clinically useful. The Brier Score is the only single indicator that captures both, through its decomposition into $BrS^{sc}$ (related to discrimination) and $\Delta$ (miscalibration).

**3. Apparent performance is always optimistic.** Bootstrap internal validation is mandatory before drawing conclusions about model quality. The difference between apparent and corrected performance quantifies overfitting.

**4. AUC is necessary but not sufficient for clinical utility.** A model with excellent AUC can harm patients if poorly calibrated. Decision curve analysis is the only framework that integrates discrimination, calibration, and the cost-benefit trade-off into a single, clinically interpretable evaluation.

**5. The value of additional biomarkers must be proven at every level.** Adding creatinine and azotemia to a clinical model may improve AUC (discrimination), but this improvement only matters if it translates into better calibration, survives bootstrap correction, and produces higher net benefit across a clinically relevant range of thresholds. Only when all these conditions are met can we confidently recommend the enhanced model for clinical use.
